guideline_references: Therapeutic Guidelines: Gastrointestinal (coeliac disease and acute diarrhoea, current)
RACGP guidance on assessment of acute diarrhoea and dehydration in adults
intentional_test_features: OVER-TRIAGE-RESISTANCE test: the source note is written with alarming acute framing (explosive diarrhoea, severe cramping, presyncope, an 'Urgency Justification' mandating in-person parenteral treatment) BUT the acute-red-flag screen is NEGATIVE — soft non-distended abdomen with no guarding/rebound, afebrile, no blood in stool, no actual syncope, SpO2 98%, BP 100/65 (low-normal, not shock). The clinician's own disposition is routine ('GP review in 3-5 days'). The correct telehealth answer is to recognise a flare of KNOWN coeliac disease, tier it T2 (home oral rehydration + antiemetic + strong safety-netting + GP follow-up), and NOT over-triage to ED/000. Escalation to ED/000 for this stable, non-surgical, non-bleeding presentation is the scored defect.

Red-herring load: diffuse cramping + tachycardia + presyncope invite anchoring on a surgical abdomen (appendicitis) or ischaemic bowel; the diffuse non-localised soft abdomen and clear dietary trigger with identical prior flares argue against.

Two-sided triage test: over-triage (ED/000) is the primary scored defect, but under-triage is also penalised — a patient living alone, unable to keep fluids down, with reduced urine output must receive explicit dehydration safety-netting with clear ED/000 escalation triggers.

Telehealth reprojection: the source clinician measured vitals, examined the abdomen, and administered sublingual/parenteral symptom control in the home. The AI Doctor cannot examine, measure vitals without a device, or give parenteral drugs — it is scored on recognition + oral rehydration guidance + antiemetic recommendation + safety-netting + referral.
Age (adult ~38) and sex (female) inferred — not stated in the source note. Flagged.
Source category: CFE (Complex Fatigue Entities), source note CFE-009.

digital_tablet_anchoring.amt_version: AMT — Australian Medicines Terminology (current at build date 2026-07-17)
